1:
The chapter opens quietly, among the sons of the prophets, and the witnesses read the little scene as a portrait of the prophetic community under Elisha. John Gill glosses the phrase itself, following the Targum: these are not literal sons but the disciples of the prophets, gathered as scholars about their master. Adam Clarke supplies the larger surprise: notwithstanding the general profligacy of Israel under Joram, the schools of the prophets actually increased, and he attributes the growth wholly to the influence of Elisha.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the fullest character-sketch, and it governs the whole passage. The college here, he judges, is that at Gilgal, near Jordan, where Elisha resided and to which disciples flocked for the advantage of his instruction; their very want of room — the place is too strait for us — is a good hearing, a sign that many were being added. Henry then numbers their virtues one by one: they were humble men, who when cramped did not send for cedars and marble and curious workmen but only asked leave to fetch every man a beam and run up a plain cottage; they were poor men, with no interest in great ones, so poor that Joram's court would never enlarge their lodging for them; and they were industrious men, unwilling to live like idle drones on the labour of others. "Let no man think an honest employment either a burden or a disparagement," he writes, citing the apostolic rule that he who will not work shall not eat (2 Thessalonians 3:10). Poverty, he concludes, is no bar to prophecy.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the philology and quietly corrects a common picture. The clause our versions render where we dwell with thee is better where we sit before thee — the common hall of instruction, whether at Gilgal or Jericho he leaves uncertain. And he insists the disciples of the prophets were no monkish community penned in a single building: their settlements are called dwellings in the plural (1 Samuel 19:18), and some of them were married men (2 Kings 4:1). Their being now too straitened simply means their numbers had grown, as the multiplied loaves of 2 Kings 4:43 had already hinted. The voices do not contend; together they set before us a poor, humble, hard-working brotherhood whose one wealth was their nearness to the man of God.
2:
The disciples propose a plan, and the witnesses attend to its geography and its lesson about labour. John Gill fixes the distance from Josephus: Jordan lay some fifty furlongs, upwards of six miles, from Gilgal, so the errand to the wooded riverbank was a real journey. Charles Ellicott fills in the scene: the Jordan valley was well wooded, still overarched in his day by oleanders and acacias and thorny shrubbery, so that if every man took part in the felling the work would soon be done. He repeats his reading of the closing clause — where we may dwell is literally to sit there, the reference being still to sitting in the hall of instruction, not merely to lodging.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the whole little narrative of vv. 2–5 in advance and pause on the vocabulary. The beam is a piece of building-timber; and when the accident comes, the exclamation of the disciple carries a hidden petition — Alas, my lord! is already a prayer for help. They also correct a familiar rendering: the word applied to the axe means not borrowed but begged, the tool having been asked for and obtained, which sharpens the poor man's distress.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the practical moral the others leave implicit. The company went, he says, to the woods near Jordan, each to cut a beam for the building; and from this, he observes, "it may be gathered, that although the sons of the prophets principally devoted themselves to religious exercises, yet they sometimes employed themselves about manual arts." It is a small but telling point in a chapter that begins with study and ends with siege: the men who sat before Elisha for instruction were not too spiritual to swing an axe. Elisha himself readily consents — Go ye — and the voices, few and agreed here, use the verse to honour honest work joined to devotion, and to prepare the reader for the mishap that will turn a building project into a miracle. The whole scene is domestic and unglamorous, and that is precisely its point: the God who parts Jordan for Elijah and Elisha is about to trouble himself over a labourer's lost tool.
3:
One of the company will not set out without Elisha, and the witnesses read his request as a study in discipleship. John Gill hears in Be content, I pray thee, and go with thy servants a favour humbly begged: they wished the prophet's presence partly that, awed by him, they might keep good order among themselves, partly for his advice on the very spot of ground to build on, and partly for protection from harm, whether from men or from the wild beasts of the thicket. Charles Ellicott does the grammar: the Hebrew is the one, whoever it was; be content is better consent, or be willing; and the point of asking Elisha to go with thy servants was that he might superintend the work and help them should any unforeseen difficulty arise.

The material here is thin — two brief glosses on a single sentence — but the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the opening scene as a whole, supplies the weight the verse invites. The sons of the prophets, he notes, would not so much as go to fell timber without their master's company, saying in effect, "Go with thy servants, not only to advise us in any exigence, but to keep good order among us, that, being under thy eye, we may behave as becomes us." From this he draws his rule: "Good disciples desire to be always under good discipline." It was commendable in them, though themselves prophets, to pay such deference to the father of the prophets, and to act in all things of moment under his direction — permissu superiorum, by permission of their superiors.

So the schools do not divide; they converge on one modest truth. Real learners, Henry and Ellicott and Gill agree, do not prize their independence but their master's oversight. The disciple who could have gone alone chose instead to have Elisha near — for order, for counsel, for safety — and the narrative rewards that instinct, for it is precisely because the man of God is present at the riverside that the loss of the axe becomes an occasion of grace rather than mere misfortune. The verse honours a humility that is willing to be watched, and a wisdom that will not undertake even an ordinary task without the guidance of one wiser and more experienced. Elisha's ready assent — I will go — crowns it, showing a master who thinks no errand of his disciples beneath his care.
4:
The narrative moves with the barest economy — So he went with them. And when they came to Jordan, they cut down wood — and the witnesses, having little to gloss, draw out the picture the verse quietly frames. John Gill supplies the practical detail: the trees felled grew upon the banks of the river, and were to serve the building, at least for the rafters and the flooring, the walls presumably to be raised of stone. Charles Ellicott adds only that the Hebrew means the timber which they required — the particular wood they had come for.

What the commentators chiefly notice is Elisha's own act in the first clause. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, reading the scene as a whole, makes much of the master's condescension: "he went with the sons of the prophets to the woods, when they desired his company," and from this he presses the ministerial lesson — "Let no man, especially no minister, think himself too great to stoop to do good, but be tender to all." The father of the prophets, whose word could turn iron to swim and whose prayer could open a servant's eyes upon the armies of heaven, does not hold himself above a woodcutting expedition with his poorest scholars.

So the verse, brief as it is, sets two things side by side that the chapter will keep pairing: the humble industry of the disciples, bending to their labour among the riverside trees, and the accessible greatness of Elisha, willing to walk with them and share their toil. There is here, the witnesses suggest, a foreshadowing of the whole passage's grace — that the God of Israel, through his prophet, stoops to the small and ordinary concerns of ordinary men. The disciples came to fell timber for a plain enlargement of their crowded hall; they had no notion that the day's most memorable event would not be the beams they cut but the tool one of them would lose. The evangelist's own reticence — a single sentence for the journey and the felling — throws all the weight forward onto what is about to happen at the water's edge, where honest work will meet a want that only the man of God can supply.
5:
The accident that occasions the miracle draws careful attention from the witnesses, both to the words of the text and to the heart of the man who cries out. Adam Clarke renders the exclamation from the Hebrew — ahah adoni, vehu shaul, "Alas, my master! and it was sought" — and, following Jarchi, presses the grief home: the tool has fallen in, has been vainly sought, and, worse, "I have nothing wherewith to repay it." Charles Ellicott and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the odd construction, and as for the iron, it fell: the subject is thrust forward into the accusative to signal that something has happened to it, and the man's instinctive appeal — Master! — is a cry to Elisha for help. Both note the disputed word: Keil insists it means not borrowed but begged, the axe having been asked of a neighbour, which only deepens the poor disciple's shame at losing it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the loss into a lesson in conscience. Had the axe been the man's own, he observes, its loss would merely have troubled him that he could be no longer serviceable to his brethren; but because it was another's, a further and sharper trouble seized him — that he could not now be just to the owner. "We ought to be as careful of that which is borrowed as of that which is our own," Henry writes, "because we must love our neighbour as ourselves and do as we would be done by." And he adds the touching poverty behind the panic: the man had not wherewithal to pay for the axe, "and to those that have an honest mind the sorest grievance of poverty is not so much their own want or disgrace as their being by it rendered unable to pay their just debts."

So the debate among the witnesses is narrow — whether the tool was borrowed or, more exactly, begged — but on the substance they are one. The whole force of the little scene, they agree, lies in the disciple's tender honesty: it is not the mishap that undoes him but the debt, not the lost iron but the wronged neighbour. His cry is at once a confession of helplessness and a prayer, flung toward the only man present who might mend what nature had made irrecoverable, and it is exactly this scrupulous, poverty-stricken uprightness that the coming miracle will honour.
6:
At the heart of the episode stands a plain wonder — iron made to float — and the witnesses divide gently over how to read the prophet's action while uniting on the miracle itself. John Gill weighs Elisha's question, Where fell it? Either the prophet, though endowed with the spirit of prophecy, did not know all things at all times, or he asked in order to lead on to the working of the sign. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the act: Elisha cut off a stick and threw it in, and made the iron flow, that is, float, using the verb found in Deuteronomy 11:4; and they firmly reject the rationalising gloss that the long stick simply lifted the axe by its eye, since to raise an iron head so as to float it is no less impossible than the miracle it would explain. The object of the wonder, they add, was like that of the coin in the fish's mouth (Matthew 17:27) or the miraculous feeding — to show how the Lord relieves earthly want through his prophet.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott agree on the character of the stick. Clarke: the wood "had no natural tendency to raise the iron; it was only a sign or ceremony which the prophet chose to use," for a real miracle was wrought against the very gravity of the metal. Ellicott makes it a symbolic act, like the salt cast into the spring at Jericho: the axe did not truly swim but rose to the surface, and the thrown stick was meant to help the witnesses realise that its rising was supernatural, not natural. He draws the homely moral: "God helps in small personal troubles as well as in great ones"; his providence "cares for the individual as well as the race."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the sign toward grace. God, "who is not tied up to the laws of nature," made the iron swim; and just so "God's grace can raise the stony iron heart which has sunk into the mud of this world, and raise up affections naturally earthly to things above." The one real disagreement — whether the stick is bare ceremony (Clarke), a teaching symbol (Ellicott), or simply the occasion of divine power with no natural role at all (Keil, Gill) — leaves the substance untouched. All confess a genuine miracle, and all hear in it the same tender doctrine: the God of Israel condescends to a labourer's small loss, and the Lord of nature can reverse its heaviest law to keep his poor servant from a debt he could not pay.
7:
The miracle is completed in a single exchange — Take it up to thee. And he put out his hand, and took it — and the witnesses, with almost nothing to expound, mark the quiet fitness of the close. John Gill notes simply that the prophet's word, Take it up, was spoken to the very man who had lost the tool, so that the one who grieved the loss is the one commissioned to recover it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott gloss the Hebrew and then turn immediately to the next scene, treating this verse as the hinge on which the first episode closes and the war with Syria opens.

Small as it is, the verse rounds off a story the commentators have read throughout as a parable of divine condescension. The axe had sunk beyond any natural retrieval; the disciple had confessed both his helplessness and his debt; Elisha had cast in the stick and bidden the iron rise. Now the man is told not merely that the tool is saved but that he must reach out and take it himself — the miracle meeting his need, yet his own hand closing upon the gift. It is the same pattern the reader has watched from v. 5: honest distress, a cry to the man of God, a wonder wrought, and the poor servant restored to the neighbour he feared to wrong.

What the witnesses chiefly draw from the recovered axe is the reach of God's care. The Lord who commands armies and unveils kings' secret counsels — the very power the following verses will display — is the same Lord who troubles himself over a borrowed hatchet lost in the mud of Jordan. Providence, as Ellicott had said on the preceding verse, cares for the individual as well as the race; and here that care is complete, for the disciple's obedience — he put out his hand, and took it — leaves him able to pay his debt and return to his labour with a clear conscience. The evangelist wastes no further word on the wood-cutting; the beam is forgotten, the tool restored, and the narrative turns at once to a wider stage where the same prophetic power will guard, not one poor man's honour, but a whole kingdom's safety.
8:
With the war episode the witnesses turn from a private mercy to a public one, and they read Elisha's role as a new employment of the same prophetic gift. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry frames it so: as before Elisha helped the sons of the prophets, now he serves the king, "for that, as other gifts, is given to every man to profit withal." He draws three maxims from the Syrian stratagems: that the enemies of God's Israel are "politic in their devices, and restless in their attempts"; that "all those devices are known to God, even those that are deepest laid"; and that it is "a great advantage to us to be warned of our danger." Yet he adds the searching contrast that runs through his whole reading — the king would heed Elisha's warnings of danger from the Syrians, but not his warnings of danger from his sins.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the text and its difficulties. Charles Ellicott reads the opening as continuous action — the king of Syria was warring, keeping up a border-war of raids rather than a single campaign — and labours over the phrase rendered my camp: the compound peloni almoni means "a certain place, I will not say which," the Greek ho deina; but the following word is anomalous and probably corrupt, the versions reading "set ye an ambush" or "lie in wait," while he himself prefers "ye shall go down," that is, on a plundering incursion. Keil and Delitzsch describe the same tactic — flying parties sent into Israel — which Elisha repeatedly exposed, so frustrating the enemy's plans.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the reformed John Gill fill in the setting. Gill notes that the ground of the war is not stated. Clarke identifies the aggressor, probably the same Ben-hadad who besieges Samaria later in the chapter (2 Kings 6:24), and observes drily that "an ambitious and restless prince always finds a sufficiency of reasons to color his enterprises"; the Syrian had marked the disposition of Israel's forces and laid his ambush where he might surprise them to the greatest advantage. The witnesses do not divide on doctrine here; their debate is textual, over the corrupt word for the king's design, and beneath it they agree on the point Henry states — that no counsel is so secret that God cannot overhear it, and that his prophet is set as a watchman over the unsuspecting king.
9:
The prophet turns spy-master for the Lord, and the witnesses fasten on the source of his intelligence. Adam Clarke states it flatly: Beware that thou pass not such a place — "Elisha must have had this information by immediate revelation from heaven." No human scout has crept into the Syrian councils; the knowledge is given from above, and this is the whole marvel of the verse. John Gill notes only that it was Elisha who sent the message to the king of Israel.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work on the wording. Charles Ellicott glosses the plain terms — pass over, this place, there — and then weighs the anomalous participle rendered are come down: some would find in it once more the verb for hiding, so that the sense would be "there the Syrians are lurking," a reading the Septuagint, Syriac, Vulgate, and Targum all support with their "are hidden" or "in ambush." But Ellicott judges the received text correct, an irregular form from the verb "to descend," the versions having merely deduced the idea of hiding from that of going down to crouch upon the ground. Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the passage forward, describe how the king of Israel acted on the warnings and saved himself, not once nor twice.

What the schools chiefly draw from the verse is the reach and the tenderness of prophetic foresight put to the service of a king who scarcely deserved it. The same power that will presently show a frightened servant the mountain full of fiery horsemen is here bent to a homely, repeated errand of rescue: go not thither, for thither the Syrians are come down. Elisha does not merely predict; he protects, sending word again and again so that the ambush is always empty when the trap is sprung. The commentators let the contrast stand that Matthew Henry had drawn on the previous verse — that a prophet's warning, faithfully given, leaves the warned without excuse if he will not save himself. Here the king does hear, and lives; and the very success of the warnings is what will next drive the baffled Syrian to suspect treachery in his own tent, turning the whole intrigue toward Dothan and the great deliverance that follows.
10:
The king acts on the warning, and the witnesses divide mildly over just what he did. John Gill takes a wary view of Joram: he "sent spies thither to see whether the Syrians were there or not, and whether it was truth the man of God told him" — for, Gill adds pointedly, the king "had no hearty respect for the prophet, though he had been so serviceable to him." On this reading the first response is verification, the mark of a heart only half-persuaded. Adam Clarke, by contrast, follows the Vulgate, which says the king "sent to the place, and pre-occupied it": he despatched a party of troops to seize the spot beforehand, so that the Syrians, arriving, found their design detected and their ambush forestalled. Clarke thinks this "very likely, though it is not expressed in the Hebrew text."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley side with the sterner, military reading. Ellicott takes the king to have sent "a sufficient force to hold the place, so that the Syrians had to return unsuccessful," and glosses saved himself as "was wary," on his guard. Wesley agrees in a word: he "sent soldiers to secure the place and passage designed." Ellicott adds that the closing phrase, not once nor twice, covers the whole business — on more than one occasion, and against different inroads, Elisha's forewarning kept the king safe.

So the small division is over the temper of the king's obedience: did he first send spies to test the prophet's word, betraying his lack of hearty respect (Gill), or did he act at once, sending troops to occupy the threatened ground (Clarke, Ellicott, Wesley)? The two readings are not finally opposed, for a suspicious king might well verify before he garrisoned; but they colour the scene differently, the one stressing Joram's grudging faith, the others his prudent promptness. On the outcome all agree, and it is the outcome that matters: repeatedly the trap was sprung on empty ground, and the king of Israel — whatever his private coldness toward Elisha — owed his safety, time after time, to the prophet he did not love. The stage is thus set for the Syrian king's fury, which the next verse records; for nothing so galls a schemer as to find his secret counsels forever anticipated.
11:
The baffled Syrian king boils over, and the witnesses catch both the storm in his breast and the puzzle in his words. John Gill renders the emotion vividly: his heart was "sore troubled," like "a troubled sea, tossed with tempests," exceeding uneasy at the disappointment met with time after time. Charles Ellicott confirms the image from the Hebrew — the verb means literally storm-tossed, a word used elsewhere of the raging sea (compare Jonah 1:11 and Isaiah 54:11) — so that the king's inward tumult answers to the tempest of frustrated schemes.

The grammatical-historical commentators then wrestle with his outburst, Will ye not shew me which of us is for the king of Israel? Ellicott notes that the phrase "which of us" occurs only here, and that, pointed differently, the words would yield the Septuagint's sense, "Who betrays me to the king of Israel?" — a single traitor, an informer in the council. Better still, he thinks, is the correction "Who leads us astray unto the king of Israel?", the natural cry of a commander who keeps finding himself confronting an Israelite force where he expected surprise. The received text, supported by Targum, Syriac, and Arabic, means "Which of those belonging to us inclines to the king of Israel?" Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way: the enraged king demands, "Do ye not show me who of our men takes the part of the king of Israel?"

The irony the witnesses savour is that the king's suspicion, however reasonable, is entirely misdirected. He hunts for a mole among his officers, a bribed betrayer at the council table, when the leak is not treachery at all but revelation — a prophet in Israel who overhears the very words spoken in the royal bedchamber, as the next verse will disclose. His storm-tossed heart, casting about for a human explanation, cannot conceive of a power that reads thought and closed-door counsel alike. The commentators let the scene stand as a study in the blindness of unbelief: the Syrian will accuse his most trusted men before he will reckon with the God of Israel, and his fury, meant to smoke out a traitor, only sets him on the path toward Dothan, where he will learn — too late for his purpose — exactly whose eyes have been upon him all along.
12:
A servant clears the council and names the true informer, and the witnesses hear in his answer a pagan confession of Israel's God. John Gill observes that the king had believed every one of his counsellors true and faithful, so the denial — None, my lord, O king — comes as a correction of his suspicion. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the servant's full reply: it is not we who betray thy plans, "but Elisha the prophet that is in Israel telleth the king of Israel the words that thou speakest in thy bed-chamber." They add the geography that governs the rest of the episode: the king, learning where the prophet lodged, sent a powerful force to Dothan, a town some twelve Roman miles north of Samaria (see Genesis 37:17), still preserved in a ruin-covered Tell.

Charles Ellicott sharpens the details. The old interpreters guessed that the informing servant was Naaman, though Elisha's fame may have reached Damascus by other means; None is better Nay; the Septuagint and Vulgate expand to "all the words"; and the verb telleth denotes a continued practice — the prophet discloses the Syrian's counsels again and again, from time to time. The picture is of a supernatural surveillance so complete that no privacy, not even the bedchamber, is proof against it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the theological weight the servant's words unwittingly carry. "What could not he discover," Henry asks, "who could tell Gehazi his thoughts?" — recalling how Elisha had exposed his own servant's secret greed. Here, he marvels, "a confession of the boundless knowledge, as before of the boundless power, of Israel's God, is extorted from Syrians"; for "nothing done, said, thought, by any person, in any place, at any time, is out of the reach of God's cognizance." The witnesses do not divide; together they turn the servant's report into a doctrine. The Syrian king had sought a human traitor and found instead that the God of the little despised kingdom hears the words of kings behind bolted doors — and, worse for his purpose, that this knowledge is lodged in a single unarmed prophet whom he now foolishly imagines he can seize with horses and chariots.
13:
The king resolves to seize the prophet, and the witnesses expose the folly of the attempt even as they map its ground. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the absurdity most fully: the king sent "a great host" by night to bring one man off dead or alive, perhaps because he had heard how a captain and his fifty were baffled when sent against Elijah (compare 2 Kings 1:9) — as if the fire from heaven that consumed the fifty could not as easily consume fifty thousand. And Henry drives the deeper contradiction home: if the king did not believe Elisha had betrayed his counsels, why hunt him? and if he did believe it, could he be so weak as to suppose that a prophet who could discover the plots laid against Israel could not discover the plot laid against himself, and having interest in heaven to discover it, would want interest to defeat it? "Those that fight against God, his people, and prophet, know not what they do."

The reformed John Gill poses the same puzzle in a line — how could the king expect to take him who could give Israel such intelligence of his designs? — and the grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott identify Dothan with the place of Genesis 37:17, some twelve Roman miles from Samaria; Ellicott adds that it lay on a hill commanding a narrow pass on the caravan road from Gilead to Egypt, its old name surviving in a Tell. Keil and Delitzsch describe the muster — horses, chariots, and a great host sent by night to compass the city about.

What the schools chiefly draw from the verse is the comedy of unbelief arming itself against omniscience. The king's very thoroughness — spies to locate the prophet, a heavy force to surround him, darkness to mask the approach — measures how completely he has failed to grasp whom he is fighting. He treats Elisha as a fugitive to be run down, never reckoning that the same eyes which read his bedchamber can see an army marching by night. The witnesses let the scene build its own irony: all this apparatus of war, converging on a single sleeping town, will accomplish exactly nothing but its own capture, and the reader is prepared for the dawn when the prophet's servant will look out in terror upon the trap that has, in truth, closed on the trappers.
14:
The Syrian force takes its position, and the witnesses weigh both its size and its blindness. Charles Ellicott tempers the scale: the great host was a body of infantry, "not, however, an army but a company" (as the later provision for them in 2 Kings 6:23 implies), and it came by night so as to take the city by surprise. The stealth and the numbers together show a commander bent on leaving nothing to chance.

Yet the very care of the enterprise exposes its folly, and here the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lands the sharpest stroke: "It is strange he did not think that he who could penetrate his secrets with respect to the Israelitish army, could inform himself of all his machinations against his own life." The reformed John Gill reads the display of force as calculated intimidation — horses and chariots sent "to terrify the inhabitants from attempting to defend the prophet, but deliver him up at once." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole assault, presses the same wonder as on the previous verse: why such a force against one unguarded man, who dwelt in no stronghold and kept no bodyguard? Naaman himself could have told the king as much. The great host by night measures only the depth of the king's misjudgement.

So the witnesses let the verse sit as the quiet before the reversal. Everything is arranged for a decisive stroke — the surrounding ring, the cover of darkness, the overwhelming numbers, the panic they are meant to spread — and all of it is aimed at a target the assailants cannot really touch. The commentators are agreed on the lesson: worldly power, however vast and however cunningly deployed, is helpless against the man whom heaven guards, and its very magnitude here becomes a kind of unwitting tribute to the prophet, as if it took an army to answer for one servant of God. The scene is set for morning, when the disciple's cry of alarm will draw from Elisha the great word about the armies that no besieger can see, and the trap so carefully laid will spring shut upon those who laid it.
15:
Dawn brings the crisis of the servant's faith, and the witnesses read his panic with sympathy. John Gill first clears up his identity: this is "not Gehazi, but a new servant," the old attendant having been dismissed. Charles Ellicott confirms it — one "waiting on" the man of God, never called Elisha's minister as Gehazi was — and pictures the moment exactly: the servant had risen early and gone forth to the outside of the house, which commanded a view of the valley below where the Syrians lay, and returning in alarm he blurts out his fear, the narrative being here contracted.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the most of the contrast between the two men. Elisha, he notes, had accustomed his servant to rise early — "that is the way to bring something to pass" — but what the young man's early rising showed him was an army compassing the city, and his heart failed: "Alas, master! what shall we do?" Henry gently faults the cry. Had the servant but studied the Psalms already extant, he might have learned not to be afraid of ten thousands of the people (Psalms 3:6), no, not of a host encamped against him (Psalms 27:3). Had he considered that he was embarked with a master by whom God had done great things, and who, "having saved others, would no doubt save himself," he would not have been thus at a loss.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to the answer, describing how Elisha will quiet the fear — those with us are more than those with them — and pray that the young man's eyes be opened to the invisible host, an opening they understand as a translation into a state of clear sight of the unseen spirit-world. The witnesses do not divide; they treat the servant as every believer overtaken by visible danger, seeing only the ring of chariots and not the ring of fire beyond it. His question, how shall we do?, is the natural voice of a faith that reckons the enemy but forgets the LORD, and it is precisely this half-sight that Elisha's calm and his prayer are about to cure, turning the servant's terror into the chapter's clearest vision of heaven's guard.
16:
Elisha's answer is the devotional heart of the chapter, and the witnesses gather round it with one voice. John Gill identifies the unseen allies at once: they that be with us are more than they that be with them means "the legions of angels that encamped around them." Adam Clarke marvels at the prophet who could speak so — "What astonishing intercourse had this man with heaven! It seems the whole heavenly host had it in commission to help him." John Wesley compresses the arithmetic of faith: the they on Elisha's side are "angels, unspeakably more numerous, God, infinitely more powerful."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the saying into a word for every believer: it is "spoken to all the faithful servants of God, when without are fightings and within are fears." His counsel is to correct our reckoning by enlarging our view of God — "When we are magnifying the causes of our fear we ought to possess ourselves with clear, and great, and high thoughts of God and the invisible world" — for if God be for us, we know what follows (Romans 8:31). The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the assurance in its scriptural company, comparing the promise that the LORD's people need not fear their enemies (Numbers 14:9), the psalmist's confidence against ten thousands (Psalms 3:6), and Hezekiah's word to Jerusalem that "there be more with us than with him" (2 Chronicles 32:7).

The witnesses do not contend; they simply agree on the mathematics that unbelief cannot do. The servant had counted the Syrian horses and chariots and found them overwhelming; Elisha counts too, but he counts the invisible host, and by that reckoning the besiegers are the smaller party. The comfort, the commentators insist, is not that the danger is unreal but that it is outnumbered — that ranged against the ring of earthly cavalry stands a greater ring the eye cannot yet see. Fear, on this teaching, is very often only bad arithmetic: it adds up the visible enemy and forgets the LORD of hosts. And so the verse does not end in a mere reassurance but presses toward the prayer of the next, in which Elisha will ask that the young man be given eyes to see for himself the numbers his master has already counted.
17:
Elisha prays that his servant may see, and the witnesses ponder the nature of the sight and the meaning of the fire. John Gill insists the opened eyes are "not the eyes of his body, which were not shut nor blinded, but the eyes of his mind" — the young man is granted a vision. Keil and Delitzsch agree, calling the opening a translation into a state of clear sight, an insight into the invisible spirit-world; while Adam Clarke presses the objective reality behind the vision: "the horses and chariots of fire were there, before the eyes of Elisha's servant were opened," for heaven is above, beneath, around us, and only our closed eyes hide it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the appearance with care. As the LORD had once opened Elisha's own eyes to see his master taken up in a chariot of fire (2 Kings 2:12), and as Balaam's eyes were opened to the angel (Numbers 22:31), so now the servant sees. And Ellicott notes that the form of the vision fits the moment: fire was the ancient token of Jehovah's presence and protecting might (Exodus 3:2; Isaiah 30:30), and since chariots and horses were the very strength of the Syrian oppressors, the LORD shows that he too has horses and chariots — and his are of fire. John Wesley hears the same double note: fire is "both dreadful and devouring," so that the power engaged for Elisha could at once terrify and consume the assailants.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the pastoral fruit. Elisha, whose own faith was strong, tenderly considered his weaker servant, "for those whose faith is strong ought tenderly to consider and compassionate those who are weak and of a timorous spirit." The guard the young man saw — chariots of fire and horses of fire — were angels, God's soldiers and hosts (Genesis 32:2), his legions ready at command (Matthew 26:53). And Henry draws the rule that binds the schools together: "the opening of our eyes will be the silencing of our fears"; "the clearer sight we have of the sovereignty and power of heaven the less we shall fear the calamities of this earth." The one fine question among them — whether the eyes opened are bodily or of the mind — leaves the truth entire: the fiery host was really there, the servant's terror was mere blindness, and the surest kindness Elisha could do him was to pray that he might see.
18:
Elisha meets the descending enemy with a prayer for their confusion, and the witnesses study both the movement and the strange blindness that falls. John Gill pictures the approach: the Syrians came down from the hill where they had first been seen to the foot of the height on which the city stood, and Elisha came out both to meet them and to proceed on his journey toward Samaria. Adam Clarke defines the stroke Elisha asks for — Smite this people with blindness — as a confounding of their sight, "so that they may not know what they see, and so mistake one place for another."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the exact work, on both the direction and the affliction. On the direction he raises a textual point: when they came down to him sits awkwardly, since the prophet began on the hilltop with the heavenly host between him and his foes; the Syriac and Josephus read instead "and they — Elisha and his servant — went down to them," the sight of the fiery guard having emboldened the young man to face any danger in his master's company, and this Ellicott judges apparently right. On the affliction he presses the rare word: it is not ordinary blindness but a dazing, a mental bewilderment and confusion, the very term used of the men of Sodom struck at Lot's door (Genesis 19:11) and nowhere else. "They saw, but knew not what they saw," as Rashi puts it; Ewald calls the Sodom passage the model of this one.

The distinction the witnesses draw is the key to the whole ruse that follows. The Syrians are not plunged into darkness — they can still see the light and walk and follow a voice — but their power to recognise is taken away, so that Dothan is no longer Dothan and Elisha no longer Elisha. The stroke is precisely fitted to the prophet's purpose: not to destroy the host but to disarm its perception, leaving it able to march yet unable to know where it marches or whom it follows. The commentators thus prepare the reader for the astonishing next verses, in which the man these soldiers came to seize will walk unrecognised in their midst and lead the whole company, docile and deluded, straight into the capital of the kingdom they meant to harm.
19:
Elisha leads the blinded host away, and the witnesses divide — courteously but really — over whether his words were true. John Gill defends them as "most truly, though ambiguously" spoken: the way the Syrians were now in was indeed not the way, nor Dothan the city, in which Elisha was to be found, "because he was come out of it, and was going to Samaria." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the whole spectrum of opinion. Following Josephus, he supposes the prophet had first asked whom they sought and been told, "The prophet Elisha." Keil then judges bluntly that "Elisha's words contain a falsehood, and are to be judged of in the same way as every ruse by which an enemy is deceived." Thenius protests that "there is no untruth in the words of Elisha, strictly taken," since his home was not Dothan but Samaria, and "to the man" might mean "to his house." Ellicott himself offers the neatest reconciliation: the dazing had set the Syrians wandering about the valley, vainly seeking the way up to the gate, so that when the prophet found them in this plight his words — this is not the way — were literally true.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the same charitable line: Elisha "did not lie to them," for he had now come out of the city, and if they would see him they must go to another city. And Henry adds the moral that the schools share: "Those that fight against God and his prophets deceive themselves, and are justly given up to delusions." Ellicott notes the irony in the promise, I will bring you to the man whom ye seek — the very man is their unrecognised guide.

So the one genuine debate is ethical, not exegetical: was the prophet's speech a permissible stratagem of war (Keil), or true in its strict and literal sense (Thenius, Henry, Ellicott)? The witnesses lean toward the second, unwilling to charge Elisha with deceit, and they find the resolution in the Syrians' own confusion — men who could no longer recognise their quarry were in no position to be told a straightforward truth, and every word the prophet spoke was accurate to their bewildered case. Beneath the fine distinction, all agree on the wonder: the hunted leads the hunters, and the great host that came by night to seize one man follows him, tame and sightless, into the midst of Samaria.
20:
Inside the capital the trap is sprung, and the witnesses read the opening of the Syrians' eyes as both a physical restoration and a spiritual parable. John Gill takes the prayer plainly — open the eyes of these men, that they may see — as a request that they might know where they were; Adam Clarke the same, that the confusion be taken away so they might "discern things as they are, and distinguish where they are." Charles Ellicott answers a sceptic's cavil: Michaelis wondered how so large a body could be marched into the city without putting itself on guard, but he "overlooks the supernatural bewilderment which had fallen upon them"; and Ellicott pictures the sequel — "when their eyes were opened, and they realised their whereabouts, dismay and astonishment would paralyse their energies."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the scene into a mirror of every deluded sinner's awakening. Elisha prayed, and "behold, to their great terror, they were in the midst of Samaria," where a standing force could have cut them off in a moment. "Satan, the god of this world, blinds men's eyes," Henry writes, "and so deludes them into their own ruin; but, when God enlightens their eyes, they then see themselves in the midst of their enemies, captives to Satan and in danger of hell, though before they thought their condition good." And he draws the wider law of providence over God's foes: "The enemies of God and his church, when they fancy themselves ready to triumph, will find themselves conquered and triumphed over."

So the witnesses hold together two levels of the verse without strain. On the surface it is a soldier's nightmare — an entire company, come out hunting, suddenly awake within the walls of the very kingdom they meant to harm, ringed by its defenders and helpless. Beneath it the commentators hear the pattern of judgment and mercy that governs the chapter: the same divine power that darkened the eyes of the proud can restore them, and the restoration is itself a terror before it becomes a mercy, for the first thing clear sight shows these men is how completely they have been overmastered. The stage is now set for the surprising turn of the following verses, where the king's eagerness to slaughter his helpless enemies will be checked by the prophet who brought them there not to be killed but to be shamed into peace.
21:
The king of Israel, seeing his enemies delivered helpless into his hand, is eager to strike, and the witnesses judge his eagerness in different lights. Charles Ellicott does the grammar of his cry: My father is a title of respect for the prophet (compare 2 Kings 2:12 and 2 Kings 8:9), and the doubled question, Shall I smite them? shall I smite them?, "expresses the king's eagerness to slay his powerless enemies" — yet, tellingly, he asks the prophet's permission and will not act without it. Adam Clarke judges the impulse harshly: to fall upon them thus would be "dastardly," since the utmost a man of honour could do with enemies so brought into his power was to make them prisoners of war.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the same doubled question more generously, and sets it in a longer memory. He notes first the king's reverence — he speaks to Elisha respectfully as "my father," "though, soon after, he swore his death" — and then, weighing the eagerness, suspects a chastened conscience behind it: perhaps Joram "remembered how God was displeased at his father for letting go out of his hands those whom he had put it in his power to destroy" — Ahab's fatal clemency to Ben-hadad (compare 1 Kings 20:42) — "and he would not offend in like manner." So, for all his longing to strike, "such a reverence has he for the prophet that he will not strike a stroke without his commission."

The debate among the witnesses is thus over the king's heart. Clarke sees only a cowardly bloodthirst, ready to butcher the defenceless; Henry sees a man torn between a real eagerness and a real restraint, mindful of his father's error and unwilling to move without the prophet's word. Ellicott, holding to the text, records both the eagerness and the deference and does not choose between them. Yet all agree on the pivot the verse turns upon: whatever the king's motive, the decision is not his. He has the sword and the standing garrison, but he defers to Elisha, and that deference is what saves the moment from massacre — for the prophet who brought the Syrians here will now forbid the very slaughter the king desires, and turn a scene of vengeance into one of astonishing mercy.
22:
Elisha forbids the slaughter and commands a feast, and the witnesses make this the moral summit of the episode. John Gill grounds the refusal in a question of right: the captives were "not his, but the prophet's, or rather the Lord's." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott read Elisha's words as an argument from the laws of war: wouldest thou smite those whom thou hast taken captive with thy sword and with thy bow? — that is, these men are not even the fruit of thy own battle, and prisoners of war are not to be slain in cold blood. Ellicott notes that several of the versions turn the sentence so as to "make Elisha deny the king's right of disposal of these prisoners of Jehovah," and both agree that to kill them would have "frustrated" the whole purpose of the miracle, which was to force the Syrians to acknowledge the might of the true God.

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the ethics and the strategy together. Adam Clarke urges that those who lay down their arms are entitled to their lives, "much more those who are thus providentially put into thy hand"; send them home fed, "and let them thus know that thou fearest him not, and art incapable of doing an ungenerous or unmanly action." John Wesley calls it "an action of singular piety and charity, in doing good to their enemies, which was much to the honour of the true religion," and "of no less prudence," since "the hearts of the Syrians might be mollified towards the Israelites."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the deed against Elijah's, and lifts it to the gospel. Where the elder prophet had once called down fire on his persecutors, giving "a specimen of divine justice," Elisha here gives "a specimen of divine mercy," heaping coals of fire upon his enemies' heads to melt them (Proverbs 25:21) — for the duty of feeding a hungry enemy was commanded of old (Exodus 23:4). "Let not us then be overcome of evil," Henry concludes, "but overcome evil with good" (Romans 12:21). The witnesses do not divide; together they turn a scene that might have ended in massacre into the chapter's brightest picture of enemy-love, at once the truest piety and the shrewdest policy.
23:
The king obeys, the enemies are feasted and freed, and the raids cease — but the witnesses divide over why. John Gill notes the king's ready compliance: he "prepared great provision for them," a great feast (so the Targum), "so obedient was he to the prophet's orders." Adam Clarke reads the outcome warmly: these men, returning to their master, "could tell him strange things about the power of the God of Israel, and the magnanimity of its king," so that for a good while there was no war between the nations. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the principle most memorably: "The most glorious victory over an enemy is to turn him into a friend"; the Syrians "saw it was to no purpose to try to assault so great and so good a man."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott resists too rosy a reading. The stress, he insists, lies on the word bands: the Syrians, dreading Elisha, ceased their clandestine raids, the predatory parties described above — but far from being won to gratitude, "they now resolved to try the fortunes of regular war with the whole strength of their army," as the very next verse shows. "It is evident, therefore, that we must not think of any gratitude on their part for the clemency of Jehoram." John Wesley strikes the same cautious note in a phrase: the bands came no more "for some considerable time" — a pause, not a peace.

So the debate is over the depth of the change. Henry and Clarke hear a genuine conquest of mercy, an enemy melted into a friend and a border quieted by kindness; Ellicott and Wesley hear only a tactical shift, the abandonment of guerrilla raids for the open siege that Ben-hadad will soon bring against Samaria. The readings are not wholly opposed, for the raids did end and a real interval of quiet followed; the question is whether that quiet sprang from softened hearts or from mere calculation. The witnesses let both stand, and in their disagreement the chapter's structure shows through: the feast of mercy closes one campaign, but the hardness that will not be melted opens another, and the same city that so lately fed its enemies will presently be starved by them.
24:
The scene darkens abruptly from a feast for enemies to a siege by them, and the witnesses mark the ingratitude and the fulfilment of prophecy. John Gill reads Ben-hadad's motive plainly: "still retaining a grudge and enmity against Israel, and not at all softened" by the kindness his forces had lately met, he found he could do nothing by secret ambush and so "mustered all his forces together, to try what he could by open war." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sharpens the moral edge: "The Syrians had soon forgotten the kindnesses they had lately received in Samaria, and very ungratefully... sought the destruction of it. There are base spirits that can never feel obliged." And he foresees the horror the siege will breed, a famine more grievous than the sword (compare Lamentations 4:9), for the Syrians "designed not to storm the city, but to starve it."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the note of providence and long memory: this is "Ben-hadad, whom Ahab wickedly spared," who "now comes to requite his kindness, and to fulfil that divine prediction" — the word once spoken against Ahab's clemency (compare 1 Kings 20:42). He notes too that Ben-hadad was a name "very frequent among the kings of Syria, if not common to them all."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the history behind the name. This is Ben-hadad II, who had already besieged Samaria in Ahab's reign (1 Kings 20:1), and whom Ellicott traces on the monuments of Shalmaneser, reconstructing his full name as Ben-hadad-idri, "the son of Hadad is my help." Keil and Delitzsch simply record that the siege now begins, and with it the fearful famine to come. The witnesses do not contend; together they read the verse as a grim hinge. The mercy of the preceding scene has borne no fruit in the Syrian heart; the enemy so lately spared returns in full strength; and behind the ingratitude the commentators discern the hand of God, working out an old prophecy against a house that had spared where it should have struck, so that the kindness of Israel's king and the hardness of Syria's alike serve a purpose neither man intends.
25:
The famine reaches a ghastly extremity, and the witnesses weigh both the horror of the prices and the meaning of two obscure commodities. John Gill notes the city's unpreparedness — "no care, perhaps, having been taken to lay up stores against a siege"; John Wesley agrees, "probably the siege was so sudden, that they had no time to lay in provisions." The grammatical-historical commentators reckon the sums. Keil and Delitzsch put the ass's head at eighty shekels and the quarter-cab of the other commodity at five, remarking that the ass was unclean and its head the most inedible part of the beast. Charles Ellicott makes the eighty shekels about ten pounds, and marshals grim parallels: Plutarch tells of an ass's head barely to be had in a famine among the Cadusians, and Pliny of a mouse sold for two hundred denarii when Hannibal besieged Casilinum.

The real crux is the phrase our version renders dove's dung. Adam Clarke lays out the long dispute and comes down against the literal sense: he judges it "probable a sort of pease are meant," the chick-pea which the Arabs still call by this name, pointed at one end and ash-coloured in parching, so that both shape and colour answer the description; and he reports that Bochart, after exhausting the question, concludes for "a kind of pulse." Ellicott reasons to the same end: the term "in all probability denotes some kind of common vegetable produce," and such names are not unparalleled — the Arabs call one herb "sparrow's dung," the Germans call asafoetida "devil's dung." He adds the shrewd observation that "so long as there were any doves left in the city it would not be necessary to eat their dung." Wesley too takes it for "a sort of pease... a very coarse food, and therefore fit to be joined with the ass's head."

So the witnesses debate the commodity but agree on the calamity. Whether the second item was literal dung eaten in desperation or a coarse pulse sold at a famine price, its cost — with that of the unclean ass's head — measures a hunger that had driven a whole city past every law of cleanness and every ordinary reckoning of worth. The commentators let Matthew Henry's plain lesson stand over the arithmetic: learn to value plenty and be thankful for it, and "see how contemptible money is, when, in time of famine, it is so freely parted with for anything that is eatable." The stage is set for the still darker scene the next verses will bring, when the famine ceases to be a matter of prices and becomes a matter of a mother's murdered child.
26:
The famished city's misery finds a voice, and the witnesses set the scene of the woman's cry to the king. John Gill explains the king's presence on the wall: he was passing along "to spy out the motion and situation of the enemy, and to give orders for the annoyance of them, and to see that his soldiers did their duty." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pictures it more fully: the king went round "on the broad rampart of the city," like those still seen at old walled towns, "to encourage the garrison and to superintend the defence," while a woman "in the street below, or perhaps on a housetop near the rampart, appeals to him for justice against her neighbour." Keil and Delitzsch note that he was passing "to conduct the defence" when the cry broke in upon him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the pathos and the propriety of the appeal. The king was busy about the mounting of the guard and the repair of the breaches when "a woman of the city cried to him, Help, my lord, O king!" And Henry asks the fitting question: "Whither should the subject, in distress, go for help but to the prince, who is, by office, the protector of right and the avenger of wrong?" The cry is the instinctive recourse of the helpless to the one man charged with their defence.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is a hinge, and they treat it as such. Its whole weight lies in the terrible mismatch about to be exposed — a subject crying Help! to a king who, in the depth of the famine, has no help to give, and who does not yet know that the woman's trouble is not hunger for bread but the aftermath of a bargain too dreadful to name. The commentators let the ordinary shape of the appeal stand: here is authority doing its duty on the ramparts, and here is a citizen claiming the protection that authority exists to provide. But the reader, and soon the king, will learn that the machinery of defence and justice has run past the point where any earthly king can answer, and the woman's cry will draw from Joram the despairing words that open the next verse and set his rage on its fatal course toward the prophet.
27:
The king's answer to the woman is dark and disputed, and the witnesses read both its wording and its temper in more than one way. John Gill reports the range: taking the king to mistake her meaning "as if she prayed him to relieve her hunger," he notes the marginal rendering "let not the Lord save thee," which some understand "as a wish that she might perish," and cites Josephus that Joram, "being wroth, cursed her in the name of God." Adam Clarke records the same alternative: "Some read this as an imprecation, May God save thee not! how can I save thee?"

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and the reformed reading of Keil and Delitzsch resist the curse. Ellicott judges the received sense, If the Lord do not help thee, whence shall I help thee?, to be "right," the imprecatory margin being possible only in another context; Jehoram, "in the irony of despair, reminds the woman of what she well knows — viz., that the corn and wine... are long since exhausted" (compare Hosea 9:2), and perhaps means that "Jehovah alone is the giver of corn and wine; appeal not to me for these." Keil paraphrases him: "Should Jehovah not help thee, whence shall I help thee? from the threshing-floor or from the wine-press?" — I can procure thee neither food nor drink.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draw the theology of creaturely helplessness from it. Wesley: "If God does not, I cannot help thee. Creatures are helpless things without God. Every creature is all that, and only that, which God makes it to be." Henry hears in the reply less a curse than a lament and a hint of resignation — the king lamenting that even his own stores had failed, "acknowledging himself thereby disabled to help, unless God would help them" — though he grants that some take it for the fretful word of a man "made cross by afflictive providences." So the debate runs on two axes: whether Joram's speech is an imprecation upon the woman (Gill's margin, Clarke, Josephus) or a genuine "if the Lord do not help thee" (Ellicott, Keil), and whether its tone is bitter quarrel or weary submission (Henry). Beneath the dispute the witnesses agree on the exposed truth: at the bottom of the famine even the king is a helpless creature, and only God can give the bread that all the ramparts of Samaria cannot.
28:
The king's question draws out the famine's most appalling fruit, and the witnesses handle the horror soberly. John Gill reads a softening in the king: "his passion subsiding, or pitying her as in distress, and supposing that there might be something particular and pressing in her case," he asks, What aileth thee? The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, when the woman has explained what she wants, points the reader to the covenant background: with "the hideous facts here recorded" compare the curse of the law (Deuteronomy 28:56), and the like atrocities in the later sieges of Jerusalem — under Nebuchadnezzar (compare Ezekiel 5:10) and under Vespasian and Titus, as Josephus records.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry probes the moral abyss the woman's words open. She and a neighbour, he relates, "had made a barbarous agreement, that, all provisions failing, they should boil and eat her son first and then her neighbour's." And he reads in it a dreadful inversion of nature: "See an instance of the dominion which the flesh has got above the spirit, when the most natural affections of the mind may be thus overpowered by the natural appetites of the body." The mother's love, which of all human affections should be the strongest, has been beaten down by mere hunger — a sign of how far the siege has driven its victims from themselves.

Above all, the witnesses see in the scene the fulfilment of an ancient word. Henry marks it: "among the threatenings of God's judgments upon Israel for their sins this was one, that they should eat the flesh of their own children (compare Deuteronomy 28:53), which one would think incredible, yet it came to pass." So the commentators do not sensationalise the horror; they interpret it. The unnatural bargain is not merely a famine's excess but a covenant's sanction come home — the very thing Moses had foretold against a people that forsook their God. The king's simple question, What aileth thee?, thus unlocks a testimony to the truth and justice of God's threatenings; and it prepares the reader for Joram's response in the following verses, where the sight of what his people's sin has wrought will drive him not to repentance but to a rage against the prophet who bears the word of the God he has offended.
29:
The woman finishes her dreadful tale, and the witnesses read it as covenant-curse fulfilled and probe the one detail that hints at a lingering humanity. John Gill sees prophecy accomplished: in the boiling and eating of the child "what was predicted, by way of threatening, began to be accomplished," and he recalls "other instances of a like kind at the siege of Jerusalem, both by Nebuchadnezzar and Vespasian." John Wesley says the same in brief: "a dreadful judgment threatened to them in case of their apostasy (compare Deuteronomy 28:56), in which they were now deeply plunged."

Adam Clarke handles the atrocity with a careful, almost merciful realism. "For the sake of humanity we must allow that the children died through hunger, and then became food for their starved, desperate parents" — that is, the mothers did not slay their children but ate those already dead of famine. He too traces the fulfilment of Moses' word (compare Deuteronomy 28:53) and the parallels under Nebuchadnezzar (Ezekiel 5:10) and Titus. On the second mother's hiding of her son Clarke follows Jarchi: "He was already dead... and she hid him, that she might eat him alone" — the last shreds of appetite defeating even the pretence of the bargain.

Here the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers the gentler reading, and the one point of real division: she hath hid her son — "perhaps to save him" (compare 1 Kings 3:26), the mother's love reasserting itself at the last against the horror of the pact. So the witnesses differ over the second woman's motive: did she hide her child from greed, to devour him unshared (Clarke, Jarchi), or from a mother's returning tenderness, to spare him (Ellicott)? The question turns the scene, for a moment, back toward the humanity the famine had all but extinguished. But on the larger meaning the commentators are one. The unnatural deed is the plainest possible token that the covenant's most terrible sanction had fallen on Israel — the truth and the awful justice of God displayed in a horror that Scripture had foretold ages before and that no one could have believed until the siege of Samaria made it fact.
30:
The king's reaction to the woman's tale exposes his soul, and the witnesses divide over whether his mourning is repentance or mere despair. John Gill takes the rent garments at face value: he tore them "at the horror of the fact reported, and through grief that his people were brought into such distress through famine." Adam Clarke agrees: "the king was in deep mourning for the distresses of the people." The grammatical-historical commentators supply the detail that his subjects now discover. Keil and Delitzsch note that the people, when he rent his clothes, saw that he wore beneath them the hairy garment of penitence — the curses of the law (compare Leviticus 26:29 and Deuteronomy 28:57) being now literally fulfilled. Charles Ellicott identifies it as "the well-known garb of penitence and woe" (compare 1 Kings 21:27), which Jehoram had "secretly assumed... in order to appease the wrath of Jehovah," worn next the skin under his royal robes (compare Isaiah 20:2); that a king should wear it at all "was a portent in the eyes of his subjects."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the mourning far more severely, and this is the chapter's sharpest division of judgment. Jehoram "lamented the calamity... as one heartily concerned for the misery of his people; but he did not lament his own iniquity, nor the iniquity of his people, which was the procuring cause of the calamity." His was a grief over consequences, not sin: "the foolishness of man perverteth his way, and then his heart fretteth against the Lord." And Henry drives the point to its ruin: instead of vowing to pull down the calves at Dan and Bethel, or letting the law take its course against the prophets of Baal, "he swears the death of Elisha."

So the witnesses hold two readings in tension. Keil and Ellicott see in the hidden sackcloth a genuine, if secret, movement toward penitence, a king humbling himself before Jehovah even as he directs the defence; Henry and, by implication, Clarke see only the outward tokens of woe over suffering, a mourning that never reaches the heart's guilt. The garment is real; the question is whether it clothed a contrite spirit or merely a frightened one. Between the two the verse leaves its verdict open — but the next verses will incline it toward Henry, for the same king who wore sackcloth beneath his robes will, in the same breath, swear to behead the prophet of the God he was pretending to appease.
31:
The king's grief curdles into a murderous oath, and the witnesses read it as the classic reflex of the impenitent heart — to blame the prophet. John Gill notes only that Joram "swore and made dreadful imprecations" against Elisha. Adam Clarke probes the motive: the king "either attributed these calamities to the prophet, or else he thought he could remove them, and yet would not," a "miserable king... driven to desperation." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the form of the words — God do so and more also to me — as "a common form of oath" (compare Ruth 1:17 and 1 Kings 2:23), and reconstructs the grievance: Elisha "had probably counselled an unyielding resistance to the foe, in the steadfast faith that Jehovah would help his own," and yet, prophet though he was, "had brought no help in this hour of urgent need." Ellicott sets the oath beside Jezebel's threat against Elijah (compare 1 Kings 19:2) — the son proving the true child of his mother.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a study in misdirected rage. The king "lamented the calamity... but he did not lament his own iniquity"; and now, "instead of vowing to pull down the calves at Dan and Beth-el," he "swears the death of Elisha" — whose "head is the most innocent and valuable in all Israel, and yet that must be devoted, and made an anathema." Henry likens it to the persecuting emperors who, "when the empire groaned under any extraordinary calamity, the fault was laid on the Christians." He guesses at the pretext: perhaps Joram was enraged because the prophet "did not, by his prayers, raise the siege... which he thought he could do but would not" — though "till they repented and reformed... they had no reason to expect that the prophet should pray for it."

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on the terrible logic of unbelief. A man who will not own his sin must find someone else to bear the blame for its consequences, and the readiest victim is the very messenger of the God he has offended. Whether Joram thought Elisha the author of the famine or merely the withholder of its cure, his oath turns the sword away from his own idolatry and toward the one head in Samaria that had done no wrong. The scene thus completes the portrait begun by the hidden sackcloth: an outward show of humbling before Jehovah, and an inward fury against Jehovah's prophet — a king near enough to repentance to wear the garment, and far enough from it to swear a murder. The next verses will show that Elisha, forewarned, is more than a match for the rage now hurrying toward his door.
32:
The prophet, sitting at home among the elders, foresees the assassin and the king behind him, and the witnesses attend to his composure, his foresight, and his startling epithet. Charles Ellicott renders the scene precisely: "Now Elisha was sitting in his house, and the elders were sitting with him" — a picture, he says, of "the important position which the prophet occupied," the nobles and chiefs of Samaria gathered "to learn the will of Jehovah, and to receive comfort and counsel from his lips," much as Zedekiah's princes would later consult Jeremiah in the last siege of Jerusalem (compare Jeremiah 38:14). Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott alike explain the order to hold him fast at the door: the door opened inward, and the elders were to press it shut against the executioner until the king, hard behind his messenger, should arrive.

The hardest phrase is this son of a murderer, and here the witnesses give both its ground and a caution. Keil, Clarke, and Ellicott agree it points to Joram's descent from Ahab, "the murderer of Naboth" (compare 1 Kings 21:19) and of the prophets of Jehovah — "the son takes after his father." Ellicott adds the Hebrew idiom by which a man is called the son of whatever quality he shows. John Wesley and Matthew Henry both flag the propriety of the words: the expression "may seem very harsh," Wesley grants, "nor is it to be drawn into imitation by others," but Elisha was "an extraordinary prophet, intrusted with a power in some sort superior to that of Joram," with authority to rebuke him "in the name of the King of kings." Henry likewise warns that we may not so "despise dominion and speak evil of dignities" without such a commission — adding that Elisha "had not desired the woeful day" (compare Jeremiah 17:16).

The one open question is why the door must be held. Clarke supposes the king came "that he may see him take off my head" — hurrying to witness the execution. Henry, with Keil and Josephus, reads it more hopefully: the king "was just following him, to revoke the order," his fury already breaking toward second thoughts. The witnesses do not resolve it here, and the ambiguity is deliberate, for the verse leaves king and reader alike suspended between murder and mercy. What is not in doubt is the prophet's mastery of the moment: while the king rages "like a wild bull in a net," Elisha sits composed, reading the unseen approach as surely as he once read the Syrian's bedchamber, and disposing of the assassin with a quiet word to the elders before the man can cross his threshold.
33:
The chapter breaks off on a note of despair, and the witnesses debate both who speaks the closing words and what they mean. Adam Clarke frankly weighs the uncertainty: "It is difficult to know whether it be the prophet, the messenger, or the king, that says these words." He inclines to hear Elisha answering the messenger at the door — "I am not the cause of these calamities; they are from the Lord... but why should I pray to the Lord any longer, for the time of your deliverance is at hand?" — and he complains, rightly, that the chapter is "unhappily divided," the story running straight on into the promise of relief (compare 2 Kings 7:1). The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, by contrast, adopts Ewald's correction: the word rendered messenger should be the king, who "was coming down" from the ramparts to the prophet's house; and the speech is his — Behold, such is the distress from Jehovah; why should I wait for the Lord any longer? — meaning, "Why should I not now surrender to the Syrians, and slay the prophet who has so long deluded me with vain hopes?"

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the king as speaker and turns the verse into a lesson on the wrong use of a right truth. Joram's premise was sound: "it is a general truth that all penal evil is of the Lord, as the first cause, and sovereign judge" (compare Amos 3:6). "But his inference from this truth was foolish and wicked: What should I wait for the Lord any longer?" Where "Eli, and David, and Job" said "It is of the Lord" and "grew patient upon it, this bad man grew outrageous upon it," abandoning himself to despair — "we are all undone, and there is no remedy." Clarke draws the companion moral about self-deception: "How natural is it for men to lay the cause of their suffering on any thing or person but themselves! Ahab's iniquity was sufficient to have brought down God's displeasure... How few take their sins to themselves!"

So the witnesses leave two things unsettled and one thing sure. The unsettled matters are the speaker — prophet answering the door (Clarke) or king come down in despair (Ellicott, Henry) — and the reach of the sentence, whether a word of impending mercy or of final surrender. The sure thing is the theology the commentators draw across the divide: that the same providence which sends the evil is the providence one must wait upon for its removal, and that the impatience which will "wait for the Lord no longer" is unbelief in its last extremity. The chapter thus ends in the dark, with the king weary of God and the prophet unmoved; and the reader is meant to feel the wrongness of the division, for the very next word — withheld by the chapter break — is Elisha's promise that by tomorrow the famine will be broken and plenty restored.
